case_name: Weller v Royal Cornwall Hospitals NHS Trust [2021] EWHC 2332 (QB) (19 July 2021)

evidence_index.text: The claimant was born on 3 August 1947 and, when on holiday in Truro, on 26 March 2016, when he would have been aged 69, he suffered an episode suggestive of a stroke.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: He was sent home from Truro Hospital and, two days later, on 28 March, when he was unrousable, he was taken back to hospital and seen in the Accident & Emergency Department.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The following day, 29 March, he was much worse, unable to communicate or walk or use his right arm, and he was diagnosed with a new stroke and was hospitalised for about six to seven weeks.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr Patel's report, purportedly addressing condition and prognosis but also in fact addressing breach and causation, is dated 12 June 2020.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr Patel

evidence_index.text: Mr Weller has been diagnosed with vascular dementia and it would appear that this is a principal cause of his ongoing difficulties and cognitive deficits.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The issue in this appeal is whether the Deputy Master was correct in refusing the defendant the facility of having expert evidence from a neuropsychologist.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Professor Wills, dated 12 July 2021. He informs the court that a neuropsychology opinion will be valuable with respect to causation because the cause and extent of the claimant's cognitive difficulties are, he says, within the particular province of a neuropsychologist.
evidence_index.speaker: Professor Wills

evidence_index.text: Dr Ian Baker confirms that, in his view, neuropsychological evidence will provide the court with the most detailed information and assessment of the claimant's cognitive status.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr Ian Baker

evidence_index.text: In my judgment, where experts as reputable and with such expertise as Professor Wills and Dr Baker tell the court that a neuropsychological opinion is required in order for the court properly to assess causation in this case, a court would with some reluctance reject such evidence as a case management decision.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: I allow the appeal and there shall be directions made accordingly.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient experienced an episode suggestive of a stroke while on holiday in Truro.

focused_exam.neurological: Mild speech disturbance and slight difficulty walking on 28 March

requestables.name: CT Scan of the Head
requestables.description: CT scan performed to assess for acute vascular event

reveal.result_summary: Extensive hyperdensity involving the left parietal, occipital and temporal lobes
reveal.clinical_significance: Diagnosis of an acute vascular event, presumed to be ischemic

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to Truro Hospital with stroke-like symptoms

timeline_phases.name: Subsequent Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient returns to hospital with worsened symptoms

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in ED with symptoms suggestive of stroke
decision_points.prompt: What should be the next step in management?

options.description: Admit for observation and further evaluation
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard care for suspected stroke to monitor and prevent complications
Inadequate monitoring for potential stroke complications

actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Defendant believed symptoms were mild and manageable

expected_action_court.description: Patient should have been admitted for observation
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence supports admission for suspected stroke

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to admit led to deterioration
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Patient should have been monitored in hospital

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed severe stroke with significant disability

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to admit for observation
Causation established - earlier intervention could have mitigated severity

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Admission could have prevented severe deterioration

tests_performed.result: Extensive hyperdensity involving the left parietal, occipital and temporal lobes
